figures.gloss: Kemetic Orthodoxy's governing theology: Netjer, the self-created divine, is one — and Ra, Aset, Wesir, Sekhmet and the rest are its 'Names', distinct persons of a single divinity; the position is called monolatry, worship of many who are faces of one.
figures.facet: A modern resolution of a genuinely ancient tension: Egyptologists themselves debate how far New Kingdom theology unified the gods (Hornung's 'the one and the many'), and Kemetic Orthodoxy settles by doctrine what the sources leave suspended — the revival's choice, worn openly as a choice.
figures.provenance: Kemetic Orthodoxy's published catechesis (Tamara Siuda, The Ancient Egyptian Prayerbook); Erik Hornung, Conceptions of God in Ancient Egypt: The One and the Many (1971, Eng. 1982) as the scholarly backdrop; Krogh & Pillifant, 'Kemetic Orthodoxy: Ancient Egyptian Religion on the Internet' (Sociology of Religion, 2004).

figures.gloss: Truth, balance and right-order taken up as the whole moral architecture of the revival: across every Kemetic current — Orthodox, independent, Afrocentric — living ma'at and opposing isfet (chaos, injustice) is the stated point of the practice, as it was the stated duty of the pharaonic state.
figures.facet: The one plank every Kemetic thread shares: communities that disagree about priesthood, race and ritual all anchor on ma'at, and the Afrocentric current has even produced formal scholarship on it — the ethical concept as the revival's common ground and its bridge back to the texts.
figures.provenance: The ma'at doctrine from Egyptological standards (Jan Assmann, Ma'at: Gerechtigkeit und Unsterblichkeit im Alten Ägypten, 1990); Maulana Karenga, Maat, the Moral Ideal in Ancient Egypt (2004); its centrality in practice per Krogh & Pillifant 2004 and Kemetic community literature.

figures.gloss: Kemetic Orthodoxy's daily personal practice: purification with water and natron, lighting the shrine's candle or lamp, incense, offering of cool water before the images of Netjer, prayer, and the reversion of offerings — consuming what was offered, as temple staff did in antiquity.
figures.facet: The pharaonic temple miniaturized into a bedroom cabinet: senut compresses the state cult's daily ritual sequence — purify, illuminate, offer, revert — to the scale of one practitioner, which is the whole reconstruction problem in one rite: the form is ancient, the private devotional setting is modern.
figures.provenance: Codified by Tamara Siuda (The Ancient Egyptian Prayerbook); the ancient daily temple ritual it compresses is documented in Egyptological literature on the cult service (e.g., Moret's and later editions of the daily ritual); practice described in Krogh & Pillifant 2004.

figures.gloss: Egyptologist-trained founder of Kemetic Orthodoxy (taught from 1988, organized as the House of Netjer): recognized by her community as Nisut — bearer of the kingly ka, the royal spirit that made pharaoh the mediator between gods and people — and crowned in rites modeled on pharaonic accession.
figures.facet: The boldest single claim in the reconstructionist world: reviving Egyptian religion complete with its linchpin office of divine kingship — where other revivals restore rites and skip the sacred king, Kemetic Orthodoxy restores the king and inherits every question that office raises in a voluntary modern congregation.
figures.provenance: Kemetic Orthodoxy's own institutional record and Siuda's publications; the kingly-ka theology from Egyptological work on divine kingship (Lanny Bell on the royal ka); movement described in Krogh & Pillifant 2004.

figures.gloss: The distinct thread that reclaims Kemet as African ancestral heritage: the Ausar Auset Society (Ra Un Nefer Amen, Brooklyn 1973) teaches a graded initiatic system of meditation, divination and self-refinement drawn from Egyptian and wider African sources, serving the African diaspora specifically.
figures.facet: Not reconstruction but repatriation: where Kemetic Orthodoxy asks 'what did the temples do?', this current asks 'whose civilization was this?' — its Kemet is an ancestral estate to be reoccupied and its practice a discipline of transformation, a genuinely different project honestly counted as a second thread, not a branch of the first.
figures.provenance: Ra Un Nefer Amen, Metu Neter Vol. 1 (1990) and the Ausar Auset Society's institutional history; Maulana Karenga's Maat scholarship (2004) for the academic wing; the current's distinctness is standard in scholarship on Kemetic movements.

edges.note: The defining revival-of seam: modern Kemetism deliberately restores Ma'at — the attested Egyptian order-concept and goddess — as its living ethical center, worked directly from the Egyptological record across a sixteen-century break.

The Ausar Auset Society is named for Ausar and Auset — Osiris and Isis in their Egyptian names — and sets the resurrected lord at the center of its initiatic system: the Afrocentric current's own documented reach back to the Egyptian shard.

Class seam of modern reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from ancient sources on a reciprocal logic — senut's reversion of offerings beside the blót's gift-for-a-gift — parallel modern constructions from unrelated ancient models.
Intra-tradition: the doctrine and its enactment — senut is where the ordinary practitioner meets the Names of Netjer daily, the monolatry performed rather than argued.

Intra-tradition: the Nisut office and the Netjer theology are one system — the community that holds the gods to be Names of one divine also holds its founder to carry the kingly ka that mediates them.

dossier.essence: Kemetism is the modern revival of ancient Egyptian religion, carried by distinct parallel currents rather than one movement: the Afrocentric Ausar Auset Society founded by Ra Un Nefer Amen in Brooklyn in 1973, Tamara Siuda's Kemetic Orthodoxy (the House of Netjer) taught from 1988, and independent reconstructionists networked online from the 1990s. Its practitioners are concentrated in the United States but dispersed globally through the internet, which sociologists identified early as the movement's primary organ of recruitment and community.
dossier.cosmos: Kemetic Orthodoxy resolves ancient Egypt's one-and-many problem by doctrine: Netjer, the self-created divine, is one, and Ra, Aset, Wesir, Sekhmet and the rest are its 'Names' — distinct persons of a single divinity, a position the community calls monolatry, settling by choice what Egyptologists like Hornung leave suspended in the sources. Across every current the moral architecture is ma'at — truth, balance, right order — against isfet, chaos and injustice, as it was the stated duty of the pharaonic state. Death practice centers on honoring the akhu, the transfigured ancestors, with the ancient judgment imagery adopted variously rather than dogmatically.
dossier.practice: Kemetic Orthodoxy's daily rite is senut: purification with water and natron, lighting the shrine, incense, offerings of cool water before the images, prayer, and the reversion of offerings — the state temple's daily sequence miniaturized to a bedroom cabinet — while the community recognizes Siuda as Nisut, bearer of the kingly ka, the boldest office any reconstruction has revived. The Ausar Auset Society runs a different project altogether: a graded initiatic discipline of meditation, divination and self-refinement serving the African diaspora, reclaiming Kemet as ancestral estate rather than reconstructing its temple service.
dossier.canon: The ancient corpus — Pyramid Texts, Coffin Texts, the Book of the Dead — serves as the source layer, mediated through Egyptology; the currents' own working texts are modern codifications, chiefly Siuda's 'The Ancient Egyptian Prayerbook' and Ra Un Nefer Amen's 'Metu Neter'.
dossier.reflects: The plural motives of revival laid side by side — the same dead religion reclaimed at once as scholarly reconstruction and as ancestral repatriation, two projects sharing a pantheon but not a purpose.
sources: Marilyn C. Krogh & Brooke Ashley Pillifant, 'Kemetic Orthodoxy: Ancient Egyptian Religion on the Internet' (2004)
Erik Hornung, 'Conceptions of God in Ancient Egypt: The One and the Many' (1982)
Maulana Karenga, 'Maat, the Moral Ideal in Ancient Egypt: A Study in Classical African Ethics' (2004)

links.cite: Erik Hornung, 'Conceptions of God in Ancient Egypt: The One and the Many' (1982)
Marilyn C. Krogh & Brooke Ashley Pillifant, 'Kemetic Orthodoxy: Ancient Egyptian Religion on the Internet' (2004)
